name: nightwatch-chat

metadata.name: nightwatch-chat
metadata.summary: The chatting application to end all chatting applications.

documentation:
Nightwatch

![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-%3E=%203.10-4b8bbe?style=for-the-badge&logo=python&logoColor=white) ![Rust](https://img.shields.io/badge/Rust-%3E=%201.60-221f1e?style=for-the-badge&logo=rust&logoColor=white) The chatting application to end all chatting applications.
# Installation As an end-user, you have multiple clients to pick from when it comes to accessing Nightwatch. Here are two of the standard clients for you to choose from: - Terminal Client ([based on urwid](https://urwid.org/index.html)) - Installation is as simple as `pip install nightwatch-chat`. - The client can be started by running `nightwatch` in your terminal. - Browser App - Available in the `nightwatch/web` folder. - Alternatively, access it at [nightwatch.iipython.dev](https://nightwatch.iipython.dev). # Server Installation Running a Nightwatch server can be a bit trickier then running the client, but follow along: ```sh git clone https://github.com/iiPythonx/nightwatch && cd nightwatch git checkout release uv venv uv pip install -e . uvicorn nightwatch.rics:app --host 0.0.0.0 ``` An example NGINX configuration: ```conf server { # SSL listen 443 ssl; ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/nightwatch.pem; ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/nightwatch.key; # Setup location server_name nightwatch.iipython.dev; location /api { proxy_pass http://192.168.0.1:8000; proxy_http_version 1.1; } location /api/ws { proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection upgrade; proxy_pass http://192.168.0.1:8000/api/ws; proxy_http_version 1.1; } } ``` # Configuration Configuration is available at: - ***nix systems**: ~/.config/nightwatch - **Windows**: %LocalAppData%\Nightwatch Client (terminal) configuration is available at `client.json`, while the server configuration is stored in `server.json`. The Nightwatch client uses the JSON for username, coloring, and more. Check the `/config` command for more information. The backend chat server uses the config file for the server name, although more is sure to come.

methods.name: nightwatch.rics.routing.files.FileCreationModel.validate_filename
documentation: Ensure that our filename conforms to ext4 storage requirements

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.User.hex
documentation: The hex color code of this user, without a leading hashtag.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.User.admin
documentation: Status of whether or not this user is an admin.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.User.bot
documentation: Status of whether or not this user is a bot.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.Message.user
documentation: The :User: object who sent this message.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.Message.message
documentation: The raw text content of this message.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.Message.time
documentation: The time this message was sent in seconds since the epoch.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.RicsInfo.name
documentation: The name of the RICS server we are connected to.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.RicsInfo.users
documentation: List of :User: objects that are connected to this server.

class_variables.name: nightwatch.bot.types.RicsInfo.chat_logs
documentation:
List of :Message: objects consisting of chat logs.
This will be the last 25 messages sent if you just joined, otherwise it will build over time.

classes.name: nightwatch.bot.client.ClientState
documentation: The current client state. Includes data such as the user list, chat logs, and websocket connection.

classes.name: nightwatch.bot.client.Context
documentation: An object to store data about the current event context.

classes.name: nightwatch.bot.client.Client

methods.name: nightwatch.bot.client.Client.__authorize
documentation:
Given an authorization payload, attempt an authorization request.

Return:
  :host:     (str) -- hostname of the backend
  :port:     (int) -- port of the backend
  :protocol: (str) -- ws(s):// depending on the port
  :auth:     (str) -- authorization code

methods.name: nightwatch.bot.client.Client.event_loop
documentation:
Establish a connection and listen to websocket messages.
This method shouldn't be called directly, use :Client.run: instead.

methods.name: nightwatch.bot.client.Client.run
documentation:
Start the client and run the event loop.

Arguments:
  :username: (str) -- the username to connect with
  :hex:      (str) -- the hex color code to connect with
  :address:  (str) -- the FQDN to connect to
The main client class, override events on this class and call :run: to start the client.

functions.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.iipython.term.color
documentation:
Colorizes text using bbcode-like markup

Parameters:
    text (str): the text to colorize
    dry (bool): dry run, only strip tags

Returns:
    text (str): the colorized text

Example:
    .color("[red]Hello, red world![/red]")
    Returns:
        [31mHello, red world![39m

    For more examples, see the Python rich library:
    https://github.com/willmcgugan/rich

functions.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.iipython.term.cprint
documentation: A wrapper for print() which calls .color() on each argument

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.Scrollable.__init__
documentation:
Box widget that makes a fixed or flow widget vertically scrollable

TODO: Focusable widgets are handled, including switching focus, but
possibly not intuitively, depending on the arrangement of widgets.  When
switching focus to a widget that is ouside of the visible part of the
original widget, the canvas scrolls up/down to the focused widget.  It
would be better to scroll until the next focusable widget is in sight
first.  But for that to work we must somehow obtain a list of focusable
rows in the original canvas.

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.Scrollable.get_scrollpos
documentation:
Current scrolling position

Lower limit is 0, upper limit is the maximum number of rows with the
given maxcol minus maxrow.

NOTE: The returned value may be too low or too high if the position has
changed but the widget wasn't rendered yet.

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.Scrollable.set_scrollpos
documentation:
Set scrolling position

If `position` is positive it is interpreted as lines from the top.
If `position` is negative it is interpreted as lines from the bottom.

Values that are too high or too low values are automatically adjusted
during rendering.

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.Scrollable.rows_max
documentation:
Return the number of rows for `size`

If `size` is not given, the currently rendered number of rows is returned.

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.ScrollBar.__init__
documentation:
Box widget that adds a scrollbar to `widget`

`widget` must be a box widget with the following methods:
  - `get_scrollpos` takes the arguments `size` and `focus` and returns
    the index of the first visible row.
  - `set_scrollpos` (optional; needed for mouse click support) takes the
    index of the first visible row.
  - `rows_max` takes `size` and `focus` and returns the total number of
    rows `widget` can render.

`thumb_char` is the character used for the scrollbar handle.
`trough_char` is used for the space above and below the handle.
`side` must be 'left' or 'right'.
`width` specifies the number of columns the scrollbar uses.

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.ScrollBar.scrollbar_side
documentation: Where to display the scrollbar; must be 'left' or 'right'

methods.name: nightwatch.client.vendor.scroll.ScrollBar.scrolling_base_widget
documentation: Nearest `original_widget` that is compatible with the scrolling API
